item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was soo confused as to why the map thing showed our solar system to be "Theta-C", then i realized (after reading to the bottom) that its because old Sol was significantly different from real life sol, and the document was suggesting that the solar system we currently live in isnt the original sol system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my favorite part. I’d love to read a tale about these guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hate those fish guys. +1

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I went ahead and set the hub as a parent page for the SCP. Should be a less intrusive crosslink option. (Not that there's anything wrong with what you proposed, just that I think setting a parent like that is always less intrusive than inline links).

item.title: Staff Post- Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
edit made by author

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show Proposed edit
- Hide

To link to the canon that was based on this article (kinda surprised it wasn't linked already) I propose the following edit:

At 00:01:01, Earth and an exoplanet designated Theta-c undergo an exchange of spatial position.

to

At 00:01:01, Earth and an exoplanet designated Theta-c undergo an exchange of spatial position.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This interested me, but once I had a closer look at the picture and realized the full implication, I rocketed my mouse towards an upvote.

Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Certainly an interesting concept, though I almost expected a lampshade of how this screws with all the other (yes, I know, no canon) space SCPs. I honestly expected someone to be relieved that the fish guys would have to deal with 2399…

+1 for interesting concept though.

item.title: Re: Planet names
item.content:encoded:
As you are the co-author of the article, I would have preferred you accepted/made a more accurate edit.

In both the fake solar system and in ours, planet are named after the Roman gods. I see no practical reason for that difference.

Thus said, your work, your rules.

item.title: Re: Planet names
item.content:encoded:
I genuinely appreciate the attempt to help there. However, that sort of edit is just a hair over the standard corrective editing you should feel comfortable doing to a piece that isn't your own. I only say that because content in this piece is referenced in an entire canon. Off the top of my head I don't know if your changes would've had an effect on stuff in any of those tales or not, but I'm also not taking that chance.

That said I've fixed this as co-author by editing it to say "…deities in Roman and Greek mythology." It's a much less intrusive edit and one I'd have felt comfortable with someone else making, should they have felt the need.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hope that the fishguys notice the humans on the Moon and take care of them at least.
After all, for beings who move entire planet, looking at such a close satellite is like looking inside their chitin pockets.

item.title: Planet names
item.content:encoded:
All planets in the Sol System were named for deities in Roman mythology. Their names were […] Hera

Hera is the name of the Greek deity, wife of Zeus. The correct Roman name (which would be consistent with the other ones) would be Juno. Fixed.

Same for Athena, in the next sentence. That cannot be fixed easily, as the corresponding Roman name Minerva is used for the unrelated space mission; however, I would suggest to exchange the names. It wouldn't be inconsistent with the real Apollo missions, as Apollo is the same for Greek and Roman myth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So… the Minerva moon colonies are still back in the other system, doomed either to the mercies of the fishguys who did this in the first place, or the slow death of resource deprivation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This doesn't seem to be worth it for all the suspension of disbelief it asks of me. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure, it's a small scrutiny, but I sense a syntactical snag in this sentence:

Over the next 12 hours, the language converts into increasingly apt English, until full communications between Earth and Theta-c are established.

Could the word "apt" be replaced with something such as "clear" or "fluent" or "understandable"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This honestly seems like a (not to my taste) sci-fi story shoehorned awkwardly into an SCP article. The object itself gets a paltry paragraph of description before you delve into the timeline. Even if I liked the story you're endeavoring to tell, you went about telling it in a kind of ham-fisted way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Swap recipes? How so?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The swap back would not remedy any of the upset caused by the exchange itself."

Perhaps not (though I really don't see why not), but it would still be a lot more simple, sensible, and believable than what they actually did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, you surely took you time for doing this. It is well made and goes cool with my headcannon. I like that alternative religion idea, based on sensory information instead of a deity. Very interesting… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I find especially amusing here is that you've indirectly created a decent explanation for the oft-scoffed-at Foundation moon base concept. This is well done overall and meshes nicely with my hedcanon. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It does ignore SCP-120 and probably others.

Even leaving that aside, the concept is interesting but I didn't care for this. Not sure why but I think the flavor of the "Everything Goes to Hell But We're Going To Trust The Most Likely Suspects" bugs me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It reminds me SCP-8900-EX, but not good as that one.
At least 8900 has specific theme―qualia, but this one is just set-up for set-up. Sorry but minus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Taste obsessed aliens stealing our solar system so the Foundation had to pull a deus ex machina and then fake the moon landings? It's just too much. It also clashes heavily with several other space-related SCPs. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit hard to understand, but I think I got it. Really unique — upvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry this is late, but minor issue with the cover-up: No Apollo missions actually put anything on the moon until 11.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was fairly neutral on this until the stinger at the end. Goddamn that hit hard, and you timed it perfectly. +1.

For every two centuries on Theta-c, there is approximately one additional Earth day over 200 years.

A tad redundant; I would suggest removing the "over 200 years".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. When the idea was fully fleshed, I realized that I'd be on the edge of "personal taste" voting. I try to make sure my execution is solid for this very reason— so those that have a personal taste that does not work for it only neutral-vote it. Thank you, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's nothing fundamentally wrong with your execution, but the concept itself stretches my credulity too thin. It's much easier for me to get behind the idea of starting over from scratch than it is for me to accept an over-write of all astronomical history (including the structure of Giza, Stonehenge, and about a million other astrologically significant sites scattered across the globe) with an extant human population still standing in the way.

Can't buy it. Even with flexible causality, changing our literal place in the universe by dozens of light-years in 1973 is too inconsistent with the rest of the body of work for me to be able to reconcile. I won't downvote, because it's quality work and it's against my religion to -1 just based on head-canon, but I also can't take it seriously.

0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We also have one… http://mashable.com/2014/10/02/pluto-planet-again/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't disagree necessarily (and not that I find that a bad thing). While it may be rare that they find inhabited planets while moving around the universe, I'm pretty sure the sole purpose of this switch was to swap recipes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They still come across as gigantic jerks to me. Sorry. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The swap back would not remedy any of the upset caused by the exchange itself. While placing Earth back in its original place, everything that had been in upheaval from the switch would remain in the same tense state, and would possibly continue to deteriorate.

Fixing the "exponentially" discrepancy now. Discrepancy should be fixed, actual value is better quantified. Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That said, it still strikes me as odd that they could not / would not enact swapback and instead went on with whatever overcomplicated effort it took to mindfuck everyone and create an entirely fake history of astronomic records and whatnot.

Not the least because in this operation here, repeatable, which it is, is synonymous with reversible.

Each day on Theta-c is just above 24 Earth hours, to an exponentially minuscule margin of difference.

I don't like the use of "exponentially" here. Also, if it's just above, then you have quantified it, then you can put forth say, what difference is there in a century, if the numbers are that small.

Ultimately, there's too much good here to downvote (especially poor stranded lunar colonists), but I don't feel like upvoting either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

I'm having trouble forming coherent sentences so, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy macaroni, this improved a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Paroxysm, this has improved so much from the drafts you showed me.

+1 with absolutely no reservations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How come this skip has access by all personnel? This is an incredibly stunning revelation that changes the way how Foundation rookies and civilians look at things. The fact that a lot of history before 1973 was a total lie made up to keep society stable won't go well with most people.

Also, how come the current reality of earth can't colonize the moon like the previous one? To prepare for another event?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so lovely. Nout enough words in my limited English to describe how I helped with my bare idea. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In response to your edited comment:

I meant to imply that Theta-c really did not have regards for what they were swapping with, and did not often come across civilized planets. Hence why the sphere is covered in a language only they speak / understand, so they can at least state at a later date "We kinda-sorta warned you, though." It's a sphere that seeks its target autonomously, rather than being guided.

In the supplement, it was stated that Theta-c showed reluctance, again setting this feeling of "Okay, we warned you, but okay." It was also stated that Theta-c showed some reluctance to continue this practice of planetary exchange, though, they'll probably go on with their habits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so ballsy, I can't help but upvote. The alternate history/cosmology stuff was well constructed, and dammit the old system had a Pluto. D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Image resolved to state Theta-c.
The diagram depicts the Sol as Earth's original system, and Theta-c's old system as its present system. Theta-3 was an error from a previous iteration of this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. Object class objection withdrawn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, I'm not finished with the article, but I'm a bit confused.

First off, the picture gives a model of a Theta-3 system, yet you keep referencing a Theta-C. I'm sure you meant for C to be 3, but this should probably be cleaned up.

Also, there is not one place in the text (or at least no in the description) of what exactly Theta-3 and Sol-1 is. As you can imagine, this makes reading about them a bit difficult.

This looks like it could be good, but no matter what, I will not upvote this until you have addressed the problems above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My reasoning for leaving it at keter is that it's not like it couldn't be done again - there /is/ the possibility that they get swapped again, say, if Theta-c decides to backtrack / take another route for any reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While this is generally interesting, there is a pet peeve that prevents me from upvoting: there is no way, short of a humanity-reset button (which is exactly what happens in this SCP, evidently) in an Ennui-5 scale that something like this could remain hidden. Several hundreds of years of astronomical development, written register, background cultural impact and beyond, erased by the Foundation? Edit: I mean, even constellations? Damn. Just damn.

It strains my credibility. A lot. I mean, one thing is to contain information about the events that transpire on a specific year in history; the other is to contain all data and memories of events prior to that year. Saying "woah, reality reconfiguration event" is only going to strain my credibility even further… but that only has to do with me being a sucker for both concepts and not wanting to see them abused.

Finally, a secondary problem. Keter? It should be considered Neutralized at this point, right?

Otherwise, I can understand why people would like (and love) this concept. I don't dislike it myself, it's engaging and all. But neutral vote. Sorryyyy :_(

Edit Edit: And yet another problem, this one is fridge logic. You are a hyper-advanced society of Utopian amphibians that have the means to swap your planet with other planets across the cosmos. What do?

Why, choose an inhabited planet to do so!

That's something only an absolute asshole of a civilization would do, imho. Not to mention that if you have this kind of power you could be using to move the population, spending a lot less energy in the process, but the tech issues underlying to the item itself are irrelevant for this article, that focus on the consequences of the swap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You combined my two favorite types of article- space and politics. U da bes, Paro.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ca-caw. Pleasure working with you sir. Quite like how this turned out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Big thanks to Cimmerian, my collaborator on this article.

Thanks to kaktus for review and for the original image!

Thank you to Djoric, Silber, TwistedGears, Tagliafierro, CryogenChaos and others, for reviewing and encouraging the drafting process.

This is related to the post that I claimed from Fausto in the Bright Challenge thread. The premise was a year that disappeared from history. (And, yeah, it has evolved a lot since we started at a generic apocalypse.)
